subject: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary:
Reviewer David requested allowing only folio allocations from private memory nodes, citing that page allocations are written in terms of page allocations and rewriting interfaces would introduce boilerplate code.

Reviewer David expressed concerns about the feasibility of disallowing page allocation and limiting it to folios, citing that folio allocations are written in terms of page allocations and would require significant boilerplate changes. He also suggested considering alternative approaches such as providing more context to the allocator or using the memalloc_* interface.

Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the current implementation of private memory nodes does not differentiate between folio and page allocations, as both ultimately call __get_pages_from_freelist(), which does not care about the distinction. He suggested adding a flag to allow slab allocations on private nodes with __GFP_THISNODE.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges that implementing a separate allocation path for private memory nodes would be too invasive and instead proposes using the existing __GFP_THISNODE flag to allow slab allocations on the private node, while also providing a new userland driver (anondax) to test the functionality without specialty hardware.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author addresses David's concern about the inability to prevent hugetlb from spilling into private nodes, explaining that MEMALLOC_PRIVATE is still needed for this purpose and can be repurposed for Brendan's needs as well.
reviews.raw_body:
Ok, this was easier to test than I expected, and hugetlb is indeed a
stickler.  We can't get there 100% with just MEMALLOC_FOLIO, we still
need a MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - specifically because of users like hugetlb.

hugetlb uses __GFP_THISNODE to do its allocations, and all hugetlb
allocations are folio allocations - so the code you shared by itself
does not gate hugetlb from spilling into private nodes.

That means we still need something like this in hugetlb:

  if (node_is_private(nid))
      /* fail allocation */

HOWEVER... if you have MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - you make the allocation
failure a *page allocator* problem, and it serves exactly the same
purpose that __GFP_PRIVATE did.

the resulting code is two lines in my anondax driver:

    unsigned int priv_flags = memalloc_private_save();
    ret = do_anonymous_page_node(vmf, dev_dax->target_node);
    memalloc_private_restore(priv_flags);

No special hugetlb, slab, arch code handling - they all just fail
to allocate / fall back.  If they fail - it means that code is using
a bad nodemask and we need to go fix it (exactly what we want!)

I think additionally, we might be able to repurpose MEMALLOC_PRIVATE
flag for Brendan's needs as well [1].

Their goal (IIRC) was to have a pile of unmapped blocks that could
be opportunistically converted to normal memory, but otherwise left
unmapped and sitting in the buddy.

Same thing - different filter point (blocks vs nodes).

If you set MEMALLOC_PRIVATE - it makes private node allocations
possible, and "private block" access (without conversion) possible.

Otherwise private nodes are unreachable, and private blocks would be
treated like CMA (last-resort stealing, lazy-direct-mapping).

And they stack (private blocks on private nodes :V).

I don't have enough time looking at his proposal, but it seems like we
can kill two birds with one stone on this.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/agYJcRgOHho8upVv@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/

~Gregory

2026-06-10.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Private Memory Nodes, which allows for isolation of memory allocation on a per-node basis. The goal is to enable features like compressed RAM and improve system resource management. However, the patch also raises questions about how to limit page allocations to folio allocations or disallow slab allocations on private nodes. It appears that implementing such restrictions would be complex and may not provide significant benefits.
